gold.rationale: The article is grounded in a real triple killing in Wayne County and genuine family and memorial coverage, but it misleadingly centers the trailhead as if it were closely tied to the main investigative focus. That impression is reinforced by an edited image adding sheriff-style tape, creating a false contextual link between a memorial scene and law-enforcement activity.

retrieved_evidence.text: A makeshift memorial has appeared at a popular trailhead in Wayne County in the days following the deaths of three women in the area, local residents reported. According to people who visit the trail regularly, mourners have placed flowers, candles and handwritten messages near the entrance, turning the site into a visible gathering point for remembrance. Community members said the memorial has gradually grown as more people come to pay their respects, reflecting ongoing concern and sympathy for the victims and their families.

Three women were killed in a triple homicide in Wayne County.

https://kutv.com/news/local/photos-three-women-found-dead-in-wayne-county-triple-homicide

There’s a growing memorial for Linda Dewey, Natalie Graves, and Margaret Oldroyd at this Wayne County trailhead. We’ve been talking with friends about what they meant to the community. Working on that story for @fox13 at 4 and 5.

According to information provided by local officials this week, the three women who died in a recent incident in Wayne County have been identified as Linda Dewey, Natalie Graves and Margaret Oldroyd. Investigators said the names were released after notifying close relatives, emphasizing that making the identities public was an important step in keeping the community informed. Authorities added that they are still piecing together the events leading up to the deaths and are asking residents who may have seen anything unusual in the area to contact investigators.

The trail area in Wayne County associated with the killings has a sparse, desert-like setting with open ground, low shrubs and a single prominent tree that has become a focal point for public gatherings. As flowers, candles and notes have accumulated around the trunk, the site has turned into an informal space for vigils where neighbors and relatives of the three women pause to reflect on the events. In recent accounts, observers describe this memorial tree and the surrounding paths as a physical marker of the community’s grief and a reminder of the unresolved questions surrounding the deaths.

Relatives of two of the women who died in the recent Wayne County incident appeared before reporters on Friday to deliver a brief public statement, according to local media accounts. Family members of Linda Dewey and Natalie Graves expressed their grief and thanked community members for the support they have received in the past few days. They also urged anyone with information that might assist the investigation to come forward, while asking that the privacy of those affected be respected as they continue to cope with the loss.

Authorities in Wayne County said in statements released in recent days that three women were found dead at a local site, and the case is being handled as a major crime investigation. Local law enforcement confirmed that the incident occurred earlier this week in a rural part of the county, triggering a large response from multiple agencies. Officials have not disclosed many details about the circumstances of the deaths, citing the ongoing nature of the inquiry, but noted that additional information may be shared once preliminary investigative steps are completed.

Wayne County authorities and local residents have focused attention on a trailhead where three women were recently found dead, with the dry terrain and scattered vegetation contrasting sharply with the emotional weight now attached to the site. In recent reports, a makeshift memorial has developed around a tree near the trail entrance, with flowers, handwritten messages, and personal mementos hanging from its branches, while yellow police tape in part of the area underscores the ongoing nature of the investigation and the continuing presence of law enforcement at the location.

One of the women killed in Wayne County has been remembered through widely shared family portraits taken outdoors, showing her in relaxed surroundings and emphasizing her role within her extended family. According to relatives who spoke publicly alongside the families of Linda Dewey and Natalie Graves, the victim was known for close ties to her community and frequent outings in nature, details that have been highlighted as mourners seek to present the three women not only as victims in a criminal case but as individuals whose lives and relationships were cut short.

The recent deaths of three women in Wayne County are being widely discussed by residents and local media as a possible triple homicide, according to early reports from the area. Law enforcement officials have acknowledged that they are treating the case as a serious criminal investigation, though they have not publicly detailed the evidence behind this assessment. Commentators in the community have noted the unusual nature of the incident for the region, while authorities continue to urge patience as forensic work and witness interviews proceed.

Family members of Linda Dewey and Natalie Graves gave a statement on Friday after the aunt and niece were killed in Wayne County while on a hike together Wednesday.
